subject: AP US Government and Politics
theme: The Bill of Rights protects individual liberties from government intrusion, but civil liberties and civil rights are constantly balanced against competing public interests through judicial interpretation and legislative action.
core_concepts: Civil liberties are protections from government action (e.g., free speech, due process); civil rights are protections from discrimination and guarantees of equal treatment (e.g., equal protection, voting rights).

Selective incorporation applies Bill of Rights protections to the states through the 14th Amendment's Due Process Clause — most (but not all) amendments have been incorporated, including the 1st, 2nd, 4th, 5th, 6th, and 8th.
The First Amendment protects five freedoms: religion (Establishment Clause + Free Exercise Clause), speech, press, assembly, and petition — each has been shaped by landmark SCOTUS cases.

Free speech protections are broad but not absolute: the 'clear and present danger' test (Schenck), evolved to the 'imminent lawless action' standard (Brandenburg), permits restriction of speech posing immediate threats.

The Establishment Clause prohibits government-sponsored religion (Engel v. Vitale — no school prayer); the Free Exercise Clause protects sincere religious practice (Wisconsin v. Yoder — Amish schooling), though these protections can conflict.

The 14th Amendment's Equal Protection Clause prohibits racial discrimination — Brown v. Board (1954) overturned 'separate but equal,' and the Civil Rights Act (1964) and Voting Rights Act (1965) extended statutory protections.

Privacy rights are implied in the Constitution's 'penumbras' — Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) established a right to marital privacy; Roe v. Wade (1973) extended it to abortion; Dobbs v. Jackson (2022) overturned Roe, returning abortion regulation to states.

Affirmative action policies attempt to correct historical discrimination — courts apply strict scrutiny to race-conscious policies, and the legal landscape has evolved through cases including Grutter v. Bollinger and Students for Fair Admissions v. Harvard (2023).

key_terms.definition: Constitutional protections that limit government power to restrict individual freedoms — e.g., free speech, free exercise of religion, due process.
Protections against discriminatory treatment and guarantees of equal access to government benefits — e.g., equal protection, voting rights.
The process by which federal courts use the 14th Amendment to apply specific Bill of Rights provisions to state governments, case by case.
First Amendment clause prohibiting government from establishing an official religion, endorsing religion, or favoring one religion over another.
First Amendment clause protecting the right to practice religion freely, though the government can restrict religiously motivated conduct with a compelling interest.

Three-part test from Lemon v. Kurtzman (1971) for evaluating Establishment Clause violations: law must have secular purpose, neither advance nor inhibit religion, and avoid excessive government entanglement with religion.
Government action that prevents publication or speech before it occurs — presumptively unconstitutional per New York Times v. United States (Pentagon Papers, 1971).

Test from Schenck v. United States (1919): speech may be restricted if it creates a danger that is both immediate and serious — evolved to the more speech-protective 'imminent lawless action' standard.
Non-verbal expressive conduct protected by the First Amendment — includes flag burning, armband wearing (Tinker v. Des Moines), and other expressive acts.
Evidence obtained through unconstitutional searches cannot be used in trial — developed under the 4th Amendment to deter police misconduct.
5th and 6th Amendment protections requiring police to inform suspects of their rights to remain silent and have an attorney before custodial interrogation.
Constitutional requirement that the government follow fair procedures before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property — 5th Amendment (federal) and 14th Amendment (state).
Constitutional doctrine that certain fundamental rights cannot be taken away by law regardless of the procedures used — used to protect privacy and other unenumerated rights.
14th Amendment provision prohibiting states from denying any person equal protection under the law — basis for anti-discrimination decisions.
Highest level of judicial review for laws affecting fundamental rights or suspect classifications (race) — law must serve a compelling government interest using the least restrictive means.
Lowest level of judicial review for laws not affecting fundamental rights or suspect classes — law need only be rationally related to a legitimate government interest.
Racial segregation required by law — overturned by Brown v. Board of Education (1954) as a violation of equal protection.
Racial segregation resulting from social patterns, housing, and economic factors rather than law — harder to remedy through constitutional litigation.
Implied constitutional right recognized in Griswold v. Connecticut (1965) — found in 'penumbras' of the Bill of Rights; used to protect decisions about family, contraception, and (formerly) abortion.
Policies designed to increase representation of historically disadvantaged groups — subject to strict scrutiny when race is used as a factor.

exam_tip: AP exam questions on civil liberties almost always ask you to connect a specific SCOTUS case to its constitutional clause — know not just the holding of each case but which amendment and clause it interprets (e.g., Engel = Establishment Clause; Gideon = 6th Amendment; Brown = Equal Protection).
